title: Contract-edit orchestra: roles, sprint dates, and redline queue
Conditional MAP: phased commit & pricing structure for Clearline Analytics
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Olivia Price <olivia.price@clearlineanalytics.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Draft MAP & conditional commit structure

Hi Olivia —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our conversation I put together a concise mutual action plan that ties commercial milestones to the evaluation checkpoints you outlined. High level: we can do a phased commit with ramp-based discounts and a short-term pilot to validate latency and inference cost.

Attached: Clearline_MAP_v3.pdf and pricing_breakdown_v2.xlsx (summary + token unit economics). Key asks from your side: QA signal for the load test (1M inference tokens/day equivalent) and SOC2 evidence for procurement.

Proposal summary (single slide):
- Phase 1 (30 days): Pilot — no commit, discounted hosted credits to validate integration and latency (target p95 < 350ms).
- Checkpoint A: completion of pilot + performance sign-off => one-time engineering onboarding credit applied.
- Phase 2 (90 days): Initial committed capacity (3 GPU reserved equivalent) with 10% committed discount, staged ramp to 60% of target throughput.
- Checkpoint B: If usage reaches agreed performance/quality metrics by day 60, an incremental 5% discount is applied for remainder of year.
- Phase 3: Annual commit option at 20% discount for a 12-month reserved capacity commitment.

Conditionality: discounts are tied to commit tiers and KPI validation; we can add an escape clause if a regulatory audit requires on-prem deployment, but that will change pricing.

Next steps: can you confirm procurement's preferred milestone acceptance criteria and whether you need our SOC2 package (I attached a summary)? If that looks good I’ll create a version of the MAP with explicit dates and a DocuSign flow.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
(Attachment: Clearline_MAP_v3.pdf, pricing_breakdown_v2.xlsx)

From: Olivia Price <olivia.price@clearlineanalytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jacob Murray <jmurray@clearlineanalytics.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@clearlineanalytics.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 10:07:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Draft MAP & conditional commit structure

Ben —

Appreciate the quick turnaround. Procurement likes the phased approach but we need: (1) a clearer performance acceptance test definition for Checkpoint A/B (exact metrics + test harness), (2) a cap on the maximum exposure during Phase 2 before the annual commit triggers, and (3) SOC2 documentation (we need the bridge letter and auditor contact).

Two specific asks:
- Can you limit our max spend exposure in Phase 2 to $60k/month unless we explicitly opt into higher tiers?
- For the 5% incremental discount at Checkpoint B, can you make that apply retroactively to the first month of Phase 2 after sign-off? Procurement prefers retroactive credits rather than forward-only discounts.

If we can get those adjustments we should be able to sign the MAP and move to legal review. Also looping Jacob for technical criteria (he runs platform).

Olivia
Head of Product Operations
Clearline Analytics
(Procurement: procurement@clearlineanalytics.com)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Olivia Price <olivia.price@clearlineanalytics.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 08:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Draft MAP & conditional commit structure

Olivia —

Thanks — good asks. Short answers below and attached a revised pricing_breakdown_v2.xlsx that shows spend caps and retroactive credit logic.

Responses:
- Performance acceptance: we’ll provide a test harness (load script + evaluation prompts) and define pass thresholds: p95 latency < 350ms, token-level quality metrics (WER < 4% on sampled prompts), and <=1.5% anomaly rate on throughput. We’ll include the test plan in the MAP appendix.
- Spend exposure cap for Phase 2: we can cap Clearline’s exposure at $60k/month during the first 30 days of Phase 2. If you want a stricter cap for the full 90-day Phase 2 we can discuss but this is what sales finance will allow given ramp risk.
- Retroactive credit: accepted. If Checkpoint B passes we’ll apply a retroactive credit covering month 1 of Phase 2 up to the $60k cap.

I’ve also attached a SOC2 summary PDF; if procurement needs the auditor contact, I’ll have our security team send a signed bridge letter to procurement directly.

If that looks good I’ll circulate the DocuSign-ready MAP and loop legal for minimal T&Cs. Karthik — any objections from pricing/finance? Marissa — can you prep CS onboarding tasks so we can hit Checkpoint A quickly?

Best,
Ben
(Attachments: pricing_breakdown_v2.xlsx, SOC2_evidence_summary.pdf)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>

- The 5% incremental discount at Checkpoint B is capped at an absolute 5% (not compoundable with other promotional credits).

title: Private deploy activation runway — ownership check-in and preflight
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Taylor <sam.taylor@edgehealth.com>, EdgeHealth Procurement <procurement@edgehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Private deploy activation runway — ownership check-in and preflight

Hi Sam / EdgeHealth team,

Thanks again for choosing Redwood for your private deployment. We’re starting the account activation runway for your VPC/on‑prem package and want to align on ownership and the minimal preflight to unblock provisioning this month.

Quick summary of what we need to validate before we start provisioning:
- Account & billing contact + PO status (procurement on thread)
- Network owner and egress policy confirmation (IT Ops)
- KMS/HSM key onboarding window and key rotation policy
- Required firewall ports and NAT/egress IPs for our control plane
- Service account + IAM binding list for the infra workers
- Host hardware constraints (if on-prem): GPUs, NICs, MTU, CNI requirements
- Timeslot for the first smoke test (30–60 minute window)

I’ve attached our preflight checklist (redwood-preflight-checklist-v1.pdf) and a template for the network constraints sheet. Can you confirm owners for network + procurement and share a rough date window for the initial activation?

If helpful, Marissa (PM) will coordinate the mutual action plan and sprint for the activation.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Lead — Enterprise
Redwood Inference
Attachment: redwood-preflight-checklist-v1.pdf (pdf)


From: Sam Taylor <sam.taylor@edgehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: EdgeHealth Procurement <procurement@edgehealth.com>, EdgeHealth IT Ops <it.ops@edgehealth.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private deploy activation runway — ownership check-in and preflight

Hi Ben,

Appreciate the checklist — looks good at first pass. Procurement: we are finalizing the PO and should have sign-off by 12/08.

Network ownership: IT Ops (cc’d) will own the VPC peering and egress rules. A couple of notes from our side:
- Our on-prem nodes sit behind a Palo Alto cluster and we need to validate support for 9000 MTU across the path.
- We have a corporate egress proxy for external package pulls (the proxy requires allowlist entries for your control plane IPs).
- KMS: we use a managed HSM (Entrust) and will need Redwood’s KMS integration docs and an expected key creation window.

I’ll ask IT Ops to drop the current firewall policy and expected maintenance windows. Tentative earliest activation week: week of 12/13.

—Sam
Sam Taylor
Head of IT — EdgeHealth
Attachment: edgehealth-network-svc-constraints.xlsx (xlsx)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Taylor <sam.taylor@edgehealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: EdgeHealth IT Ops <it.ops@edgehealth.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Dec 2026 14:11:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Private deploy activation runway — ownership check-in and preflight

Forwarding Ben’s thread with a lightweight activation playbook attached — I’ve highlighted the items we should confirm before scheduling a provisioning window. I’ll drive the MAP once owners are locked.

MARISSA’S ACTIVATION PRECHECK (short):
1) Procurement PO or signed MSA in place
2) Control-plane allowlist + egress IPs confirmed for your proxy
3) KMS integration window and required certs exchanged
4) Service accounts + minimum IAM roles list submitted
5) Network MTU and path validated (9000 MTU flag noted)
6) If on-prem: server inventory (GPU model, driver versions, NIC) shared
7) Access method for logs & telemetry (SFTP vs syslog vs agent)
8) Backup snapshot/warm standby plan for rollback
9) SLA escalation contacts + oncall rotation during activation
10) Tentative smoke test date (30–60 min)

I attached a streamlined preflight checklist and a suggested smoke-test runbook excerpt; please review and highlight anything we missed.

Thanks — I’ll schedule a 30-minute sync once Sam confirms owners/date.

Cheers,
Marissa Cole
Technical Program Manager — Onboarding
Redwood Inference
Attachment: redwood-preflight-checklist-v1.pdf (pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Taylor <sam.taylor@edgehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 04 Dec 2026 14:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private deploy activation runway — ownership check-in and preflight

Thanks Sam — that timeline sounds reasonable. Looping in Karthik (network infra) and Lauren (security/compliance) to start the technical validation conversation.

Karthik / Lauren — quick asks from EdgeHealth:
- Confirm the minimal set of control-plane IPs and port ranges we need to provide to EdgeHealth’s proxy teams. We’ll supply both IPv4 and IPv6 where available.
- KMS integration: do we require any on-prem certificates ahead of key provisioning? If so, please list exact formats (PEM, PKCS12) and CSR steps.
- For the MTU note: what checks should IT Ops run so we can mark the path as OK for 9000?
- If EdgeHealth wants to host the model weights on-prem, what are the recommended NIC throughput and disk IOPS minimums for the initial 2‑GPU config?

Sam — can you ask IT Ops to populate the attached network sheet with the current firewall policy and an ideal maintenance window for week of 12/13? If we can get that by EOD Mon (12/06) we’ll propose the exact smoke test slot.

Ben
Ben Carter
Account Lead — Enterprise
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Sam Taylor <sam.taylor@edgehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, 05 Dec 2026 15:48:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private deploy activation runway — ownership check-in and preflight

Hi all — thanks for the context. Short network validation plan and next steps below.

1) Control-plane allowlist (minimal):
   - outbound TCP 443 to: 35.214.12.45, 35.214.13.52
   - outbound TCP 5222 (if websocket fallback used) same hosts
   - NTP UDP 123 to pool.ntp.org (or your internal NTP)
   - DNS (UDP/TCP 53) to your resolver or 8.8.8.8 as a fallback

2) MTU validation steps for IT Ops:
   - iperf3 test across the endpoint path with -M 9000; confirm no fragmentation and <1% retransmits
   - ping with 8972 bytes (+ headers) with DF bit set to detect fragmentation

3) KMS/cert notes:
   - We support PKCS12 and PEM bundles. For HSM-backed keys we need a public key thumbprint and a signed CSR if they will sign our service cert.
   - Please send certs as PEM and the signing chain in a single file.

4) On‑prem host guidance (2x GPUs initial):
   - NIC: 25 Gbps recommended, 10 Gbps minimum; enable jumbo frames (MTU 9000) end-to-end.
   - Disk: NVMe with sustained 600 MB/s per device; prefer RAID1 for model weight availability.
   - Drivers: CUDA 12.x, drivers matching vendor recommended pairings; confirm GPU model (e.g., A40 / A100) in inventory.

5) Firewall sample rules (attached): firewall-sample-rules.txt (text) — please drop into your policy review.

Next steps: Sam / IT Ops — can you populate the network constraints sheet by EOD Mon 2026-12-06 and confirm the maintenance window for week of 12/13? Once received, Marissa and I will lock a 30–60 minute smoke test and provide a runbook.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Network Engineer — Redwood Inference
Attachment: firewall-sample-rules.txt (txt)

4) On‑prem host guidance (2x GPUs initial):
   - NIC: 25 Gbps recommended, 10 Gbps minimum; enable jumbo frames (MTU 9000) end-to-end.
   - Disk: NVMe with sustained 600 MB/s per device; prefer RAID1 for model weight availability.
   - Drivers: CUDA 12.x, drivers matching vendor recommended pairings; confirm GPU model (e.g., A40 / A100) in inventory.

5) Firewall sample rules (attached): firewall-sample-rules.txt (text) — please drop into your policy review.

Next steps: Sam / IT Ops — can you populate the network constraints sheet by EOD Mon 2026-12-06 and confirm the maintenance window for week of 12/13? Once received, Marissa and I will lock a 30–60 minute smoke test and provide a runbook.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Network Engineer — Redwood Inference
Attachment: firewall-sample-rules.txt (txt)

Subject: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Hi Camila,

Thanks for taking this on. As part of our SOC 2 audit (scope: user and admin activity) BrightShield has asked for a timebound extract of admin/API/operator activity for the period 2026-06-01 to 2026-11-30. Topline asks:

- Full change events (who/what/when) for role changes, API key creates/revokes, model deployments, permission grants
- Operator session start/stop, console sign-ins, and impersonation events
- Timestamps in UTC, event IDs, and request IDs for correlation
- Preferred delivery: compressed Parquet OR newline-delimited JSON (JSONL) with a manifest and checksums

BrightShield also wants to understand our sampling approach if we provide a rolling sample instead of full export, and confirmation of PII redaction applied to free-text fields before delivery. Can you confirm what you can provide, expected delivery cadence, and what attachments (schema/manifest) you will include?

From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-02T10:04:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Hi Rachel — appreciate the clear ask. Summary of what Redwood can deliver and proposed hygiene: 

- What’s logged (exportable fields): event_ts_utc, event_id, actor_id, actor_email, actor_role, action_type, resource_type, resource_id, request_id, ip_address (hashed on request), user_agent, kv_changes (structured, limited to metadata keys), kv_cache_hit (bool), model_version, deployment_id, trace_id. 
- Retention & scope: we retain admin/operator activity for 18 months. The requested window (2026-06-01 to 2026-11-30) is fully available.
- Export formats supported: Parquet (preferred for size), JSONL, and CSV. We will supply a manifest (snapshot_manifest_20261205.jsonl) + SHA256 checksums (runbook_checksums.txt).
- Redaction options: we can apply token-level PII redaction to free-text fields or redact entire free-text columns. We will not alter event IDs/request IDs or timestamps.
- Sampling: default is full export; if you elect sampling, we support deterministic stratified sampling by actor_role + day to preserve event distribution. Recommend sampling only for dev/test evidence, not for primary SOC2 runs.

I’ve attached the admin_activity_schema_v1.csv and a short export_options_matrix.xlsx that maps size/transfer methods (S3 presigned, SFTP, encrypted transfer appliance).

Proposed next steps:
1) Confirm delivery format (Parquet vs JSONL) and PII redaction level by EOD 2026-12-03.
2) We will prepare a dry-run manifest and checksum for your review.
3) Final export scheduled for 2026-12-05 with transfer details to follow.

Edge notes: if Legal needs an attestation letter regarding redaction steps, loop us in and we’ll produce a short statement.

Thanks — Camila
Camila Reyes
Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference

[Attachments: admin_activity_schema_v1.csv, export_options_matrix.xlsx]

From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-03T08:19:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Adding ops detail from the serving/runtime side: 

- Snapshotting: we will create a point-in-time snapshot for the requested window sampled by event_ts_utc. For large exports we stream into compressed Parquet partitions (by day) and produce a top-level manifest.
- Integrity & custody: manifest will contain per-file SHA256; we can provide PGP-signed manifest on request. For delivery we support:
  * s3 presigned (preferred for size),
  * SFTP push to your firewalled endpoint (we will follow sftp_handshake_instructions.pdf), or
  * managed transfer to an approved cloud storage bucket (cross-account).
- Redaction implementation: redaction runs in a dedicated scrub job that writes both a redacted and a provider-only unredacted version to an airgapped bucket; unredacted copy is retained ONLY for 90 days under custody controls and is deleted after the attestation window unless Legal requests otherwise.

Marcus — please confirm throughput estimate and expected job runtime so we can set a delivery ETA.

— Jordan
Senior Platform Engineer, Redwood Inference

[Attachment: sftp_handshake_instructions.pdf]

From: Rachel Dunn <rachel.dunn@medicosys.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, BrightShield Audit <soc2@brightshield.com>, Legal Team <legal@medicosys.com>
Date: 2026-12-04T11:07:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Admin activity export request for SOC 2 evidence

Thanks Camila and Jordan — this is very helpful. A couple of clarifications before we finalize: 

1) PII redaction: we want deterministic redaction so auditors can validate that the same event redacts consistently across exports. Token-level hashing with a salted (per-export) salt is acceptable if you include the hashing algorithm and salt rotation policy in the manifest.
2) Custody: we need an attestation that unredacted copies are not shared outside Redwood and the 90-day holding/custody process is auditable. Can Legal provide that?
3) Delivery: MedicoSys prefers S3 presigned links (they ingest directly). Please provide a presigned bucket path and an estimated TTL.
4) Manifest: please include row counts per partition and a sample of 10 event_ids (non-PII) for early validation.

If you can confirm the above we will route this into BrightShield's evidence intake queue and schedule the remote validation window.

Regards,
Rachel

On a related note — BrightShield asked whether Redwood logs the KV cache hit/miss flag for generation requests. If you can include that field (kv_cache_hit) that would help a separate traceability check.

- Redaction & determinism: deterministic salted hashing will be applied to actor_email and ip_address fields (salt recorded in manifest, salt rotation policy attached). Free-text columns will be redacted entirely in the delivered export.
- Custody & attestation: Legal will generate an attestation that unredacted copies are retained in a restricted bucket for 90 days and are only accessible to the evidence custodian team. - Manifest: will include partition-level row counts, a 10-event sample (event_id + event_ts_utc), SHA256 checksums, and the hashing algorithm details. We will also include kv_cache_hit as requested.

Hi Elaine — great speaking with you. Recap and immediate next steps below.

   - Primary product owner / champion: Elaine Park (you) — owns acceptance criteria

   - Security: CISO / infosec (Tom Anders) — needs SOC2 summary, data flow diagram, retention policy
   - Procurement: procurement@cardiohealth.com — contract & PO path
   - Delivery/Platform: Monica Patel (CS) — pilot onboarding and SLOs

   - Short retention (14 days) is commonly requested; we can support on a pilot basis but need legal sign-off for the change and an explicit data handling addendum

3) Pricing/discounts: we typically offer a pilot discount (one-time credit) tied to scope and term. If Procurement requires a committed pilot capacity, we can convert to a small reserved pool pricing with a short-term commitment.

   - Please introduce procurement@cardiohealth.com and Tom Anders on thread if possible

title: Re: Hosted API — data residency, retention, and erasure details (GreenBridge)
preview:
From: Lucas Rivera <security@greenbridge.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Hosted API — data residency / retention / erasure questions

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the demo yesterday. Our InfoSec team has a short set of clarifying questions about the hosted API around data residency, retention and deletion before we proceed to procurement. Can you confirm the following for GreenBridge's evaluation?

1) Where is customer data stored by default (region / country)? Are we able to request EU-only or specific-region residency for a hosted account?
2) What categories of data does Redwood persist (full request/response payloads, logs, billing metadata, metrics, cached KV/state)? For each category, please state default retention window and whether it can be shortened to 0 days.
3) How are backups and disaster recovery snapshots handled? What is the backup retention period and deletion behavior after a user-initiated erase?
4) Describe the deletion/erasure process for hosted accounts. Is deletion synchronous? Do you provide an audit trail proving data removal?
5) Are there any derived artifacts you keep (embeddings, telemetry aggregates) and how are they treated under erasure requests?

If you have a short PDF with the answers & references to your docs or SOC2 artefacts, that would be very helpful. We need this for our procurement checklist by Monday.

Best,
Lucas
Security Engineer, GreenBridge Health

--
GreenBridge Health | security@greenbridge.com


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>
To: Lucas Rivera <security@greenbridge.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 11:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API — data residency / retention / erasure questions

Hi Lucas — thanks for the clear list. Below are concise answers; I attached a one-page PDF summary and an extract from our SOC 2 report for evidence. If you want, we can schedule a quick call to walk through any item.

1) Data residency (default / options)
- Default: For new hosted accounts, request/response data and associated metadata are stored in the region you select at account creation. For US customers the default region is us-west-2 (Oregon).
- EU option: We offer EU-hosted tenancy (eu-west-1) for customers who require EU-only residency. We can pin hosted accounts to a region at onboarding; cross-region replication only occurs if explicitly enabled for multi-region apps.

2) Categories persisted & retention windows (defaults and opt-down)
- Request/Response payloads (API inputs and generated outputs): default retention 30 days for debugging and quality monitoring. Customers may opt out at account level to set retention to 0 days for prompt/response content.
- Operational logs / request metadata (timestamps, model used, token counts, latency): default retention 90 days. These are retained for billing, troubleshooting and usage analytics. We support shortening to 30 days on request for customers with stricter needs; 0 days for operational logs is not supported because we need minimal metadata for billing and anti-abuse, but we can pseudonymize retained fields on request.
- Audit trail / admin logs (console logins, admin actions): retained 365 days by default; this supports security investigations and compliance. Can be shortened via enterprise contract in Dedicated/Private offers.
- Cached KV / prefix caches (server-side model caches to speed serving): ephemeral; default eviction window is minutes-to-hours depending on traffic. No persistent long-term storage of user-submitted context in caches.

3) Backups and DR snapshots
- Backups: nightly encrypted snapshots of configuration/state (not full prompt libraries) are taken and retained for up to 90 days.
- Snapshots may include system metadata required to restore service (routing state, model version mappings). We do not store full request/response logs in backups beyond the active retention windows described above.
- Erasure behavior: user-initiated deletions remove primary copies immediately (see below); backups are sanitized on a rolling schedule and the platform's backup retention policy means remnants in snapshots may persist up to 90 days, after which they are cryptographically deleted by key rotation and storage reclamation jobs.

4) Deletion / erasure process
- Self-service: Account admins can trigger data purges from the Redwood Console (Account -> Data Controls -> Purge). This initiates immediate removal of primary stored request/response records and marks records for removal from active indices.
- Asynchronous cleanup: Deletion is acknowledged synchronously in the UI, while background jobs delete related artifacts (indices, caches, and references). The primary deletion completes within minutes; full propagation (including queued analytics and backups) completes within 72 hours for primary systems and up to 90 days for backups as noted above.
- Audit trail & confirmation: We generate an audit entry for the purge action (who/when) and provide a deletion token you can use as proof. For enterprise customers we can provide a signed attestation and a log export showing the deletion event timestamp.

5) Derived artifacts (embeddings, telemetry)
- Embeddings derived from customer content are treated as customer data. By default embeddings are retained 30 days; customers may request deletion or retention = 0.
- Aggregated telemetry (anonymized usage statistics, performance histograms) are retained for product analytics and are not reversible to customer content. If you require the removal of a tenant-level aggregate in a contracted environment, we can discuss a contract amendment.

Encryption & access controls (quick notes)
- All data at rest is encrypted (AES-256) and in transit (TLS1.3). Customer data is logically separated per account. Access to raw customer content is limited to a small set of service engineers and only for incident triage; access is logged and gated by JIT approval.

Attachments: the PDF includes a short table with retention windows and links to the console controls. The SOC2 extract shows our control mapping for data deletion and logging.

If you need explicit language for your contract or procurement form, I can provide a one-line clause + appendix wording.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0182

Attachment(s): redwood-data-retention-summary.pdf, redwood-soc2-extract.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.ai>

Date: Sat, 05 Dec 2026 12:15:00 -08:00

Procurement wants a clear statement on how backups are sanitized after a purge (they expect 'residuals removed within 90 days' phrasing). Can you confirm that’s accurate and whether we should call out key-rotation as the technical mechanism?

Also, Jonas — can you confirm whether we can offer 30-day operational logs instead of 90 as a baseline for mid-market (and whether that requires an account-level flag)?

Thanks,

That aligns with how our storage providers and key rotation processes work. Good to call out key rotation.
- Operational logs: we can offer 30-day retention for operational logs on request for hosted accounts; it’s an account-level flag set during onboarding (no infra change). 0 days for operational logs is not feasible for billing/abuse detection.

title: Performance acceptance plan and MAP benchmarking results — FluxHealth pilot
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Kelsey Ortiz <kelsey.ortiz@fluxhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 16:04:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Pilot MAP — performance evaluation scope and timing

Kelsey — thanks again for the call today. Per our discussion I put together a short mutual action plan focused on the performance acceptance criteria your team asked about (latency, TTFT, throughput, and concurrency behavior).

High level ask: we want to validate under your representative workload (sample traffic in the attached spreadsheet) that the candidate model + config meets the acceptance SLOs below across a 48-hour run before the planned go-live.

Proposed next steps (short):
- You: confirm workload spreadsheet (flux_workload_spec_v2.xlsx) and highlight any missing request types.
- Us: run baseline MAP benchmark on hosted Redwood cluster (quantized & fp16 variants), share results and detailed traces.
- Joint: 48-hour soak run with concurrent producers, compare to SLOs and decide go/no-go.

Acceptance SLOs (draft):
- 95th perc TTFT (time-to-first-token) <= 320ms for short responses (<128 tokens).
- Mean per-token latency <= 18ms at target throughput.
- Sustained throughput >= 220 tokens/sec at 8 concurrent request lanes (representative of your peak).
- Error rate (5xx + timeouts) < 0.1% over 48 hours.

I'll attach a concise benchmark plan (PDF) after we lock the workload. Can you confirm the attached spreadsheet is the right superset today?

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Attachments: flux_workload_spec_v2.xlsx (xlsx)


From: Kelsey Ortiz <kelsey.ortiz@fluxhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: FluxHealth Engineering <eng@fluxhealth.com>
Date: Tue, 01 Dec 2026 10:21:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Pilot MAP — performance evaluation scope and timing

Ben — attaching the workload spreadsheet you requested. The file contains production-sampled traces mapped to three request classes: short clinical-snippet (mostly <64 tokens), summary responses (~150 tokens), and long extractors (~400 tokens).

A couple notes from our side:
- Peak concurrency we expect is closer to 12 simultaneous interactive lanes for the clinician UI, though background batch jobs run at slower cadence (we excluded those).
- For TTFT, anything above ~400ms is noticeable for clinicians; 320ms is ambitious but worth testing.
- We will need logging of prompt/response latencies tied to request id, and a copy of the token-level latency histogram.

I've included our acceptance criteria doc (flux_acceptance_criteria_v1.docx) for reference — it's fairly high level, we expect Redwood to translate into measurable SLOs.

-Kelsey
Kelsey Ortiz
Director, Engineering
FluxHealth

Attachments: flux_workload_spec_v2.xlsx (xlsx), flux_acceptance_criteria_v1.docx (docx)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Kelsey Ortiz <kelsey.ortiz@fluxhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 02 Dec 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: Performance acceptance plan (draft) + proposed execution dates

Kelsey — thanks, that helps a lot. I pushed a concise execution plan to the attached PDF (redwood_map_benchmark_plan.pdf) and summarized the operational bits below. The attached PDF includes runbook commands and the exact measurement queries we'll use in tracing.

Execution summary:
- Baseline runs (Day 0): single-run A/B comparing your production config vs. Redwood-optimized config (quant & fp16 variants). Duration: 4 hours.
- Scale stress (Day 1): stepped concurrency up to 12 lanes, run 8 hours to collect percentiles and per-token histograms.
- Soak (Day 2-3): 48-hour continuous run at target throughput with auto-batching enabled; capture steady-state histograms and error logs.

Concrete SLOs we will measure (final):
- TTFT p50/p95 (short class): <= 180ms / <= 360ms (p95 target negotiable).
- Mean per-token latency across all classes: <= 20ms.
- Sustained tokens/sec: >= 220 at 8 lanes, graceful degradation up to 12 lanes with <20% throughput drop.
- Regression threshold: any config that increases customer-observed p95 TTFT by >15% triggers rollback.

Deliverables:
- Raw traces and token-level histograms (CSV),
- Consolidated summary (spreadsheet) with color-coded pass/fail against the SLOs,
- Short write-up of recommended config (batching, kv-cache hints, quantization profile).

Proposed dates: baseline 2026-12-08, scale stress 2026-12-09, soak 2026-12-10 to 2026-12-12. Can you confirm availability for those dates?

Ben

Attachments: redwood_map_benchmark_plan.pdf (pdf)


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Dec 2026 08:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Performance acceptance plan (draft) + proposed execution dates

Rishi — can you confirm we can provision the 4x A100-equivalent nodes for 12/08–12/12 (baseline through soak) and flag any risks (maintenance windows, quota). Also confirm who will own the runbook execution ops and the post-run artifact collection.

Thanks — we want to keep the proposed dates if FluxHealth is available.

Monica
VP, Customer Success
Redwood Inference


From: Kelsey Ortiz <kelsey.ortiz@fluxhealth.com>

Cc: FluxHealth Engineering <eng@fluxhealth.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 05 Dec 2026 11:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Performance acceptance plan and MAP benchmarking results — FluxHealth pilot

Ben/Monica — confirmed. FluxHealth engineering is available for the baseline on 2026-12-08 and the soak window 12/10–12/12. We prefer the soak run to start at 02:00 PST on 12/10 to align with our overnight batch traffic.

Acceptance clarifications:
- Please ensure traces include request ids tied to our internal session ids (we provided a mapping in the spreadsheet).
- For the go/no-go call we'll use the pass/fail spreadsheet: TTFT p95 and error rate are the hard gates; throughput is expected but negotiable if latency targets hold.

We'll expect the artifacts by 12/13 COB so our SRE can run the final acceptance checklist. If Redwood anticipates any risk of not capturing token-level histograms in the soak, call it out now.

- Mean per-token latency across all classes: <= 20ms.
- Sustained tokens/sec: >= 220 at 8 lanes, graceful degradation up to 12 lanes with <20% throughput drop.
- Regression threshold: any config that increases customer-observed p95 TTFT by >15% triggers rollback.

Deliverables:
- Raw traces and token-level histograms (CSV),
- Consolidated summary (spreadsheet) with color-coded pass/fail against the SLOs,
- Short write-up of recommended config (batching, kv-cache hints, quantization profile).

Proposed dates: baseline 2026-12-08, scale stress 2026-12-09, soak 2026-12-10 to 2026-12-12. Can you confirm availability for those dates?

Ben

title: Procurement lane decision: Marketplace listing, Private Offer, or Direct Redwood contract
preview:
From: Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T14:21:00Z
Subject: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Procurement is asking for a clear recommendation on whether we should buy Redwood through a cloud marketplace (AWS/Azure/GCP), request a Marketplace Private Offer, or go through a direct Redwood contract (MSA + PO). Their main concerns: speed of procurement, invoice and PO flow, discounts/credits, and any impact on support/SLA and co-sell benefits.

High-level context: Nimble Health is piloting a 2-month PoC (approx. $18k spend) and wants the option to scale to a reserved capacity commitment if things look good. The procurement team prefers marketplace buys for quick approval, but our legal team may request an MSA for anything above a 90-day pilot.

Can you outline pros/cons and practical timelines for each path? Also—any gotchas around co-sell credits or channel incentives we should be aware of?

Appreciate a short, actionable breakdown we can hand to procurement.

Thanks,
Samira
Samira Khan
Head of Procurement, Nimble Health
Samira.khan@nimblehealth.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

[Attachment stub: Nimble_SOW_draft.pdf]

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Cc: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-02T09:07:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Samira — thanks for the clear context. Below is a concise, procurement-ready comparison and my recommendation based on Nimble's PoC -> scale profile. I’ve attached a short pricing matrix and a checklist we use for AWS Private Offers.

1) Cloud Marketplace (public listing)
- Speed: Fastest. Customer can purchase via existing cloud contract or consolidated invoice within 1–7 business days once procurement approves.
- Billing: Billed via the cloud provider’s invoice to the customer (consolidated cloud bill).
- Pricing & discounts: Standard list pricing on the listing. Limited ability to apply negotiated discounts — typically managed via Provider or Marketplace promotions, not line-item custom pricing.
- Support/SLA: Standard Redwood hosted SLA applies, but there are limits on contractual addenda via the marketplace. For pilots, this is often acceptable.
- Co-sell impact: Marketplace listing makes it straightforward for cloud partner teams to attribute usage, but co-sell incentive mechanics vary by partner and may require a private offer to capture special credits.
- Best when: Customer needs immediate procurement simplicity and is OK with standard pricing. Good for pilots and low-friction PoC purchases.

2) Marketplace Private Offer (MPP) / Cloud Private Offer
- Speed: Moderate. Setting up a private offer requires coordination between buyer procurement, cloud sales, and Redwood (usually 5–20 business days depending on approvals).
- Billing: Still billed through the cloud provider (customer sees charges on cloud bill), but pricing/discounts can be customized in the private offer.
- Pricing & discounts: Supports negotiated discounts, custom SKUs, and term-based commitments. Allows us to reflect a negotiated pilot price or scale pricing while keeping the billing path through the marketplace.
- Support/SLA: You can include a reference to the Redwood SLA, and commercial terms are captured in the private offer; however, legal teams sometimes still prefer an MSA for broader terms.
- Co-sell impact: Best path to preserve marketplace billing while enabling co-sell crediting and partner incentives — cloud partner needs to sign off on the private offer to make it co-sell-eligible.
- Best when: You want marketplace invoicing but need negotiated price/terms (pilot discounts, transition to commitment).

3) Direct Redwood Contract (MSA + PO / Redwood Invoice)
- Speed: Slowest. Negotiation of MSA and commercial terms typically 2–8 weeks (can be longer if custom legal terms).
- Billing: Redwood issues invoices directly (or can accept customer PO per the MSA).
- Pricing & discounts: Full flexibility to negotiate. Can include committed discounts, reserved capacity, and custom SLAs.
- Support/SLA: Full contract flexibility (dedicated support add-ons, explicit uptime targets, breach remedies).
- Co-sell impact: Co-sell crediting requires coordination; direct contracts can still be co-sell-enabled but may not flow through marketplace incentive systems automatically — we typically set a co-sell arrangement in parallel.
- Best when: Customer needs contractual protections, custom SLAs, or wants Redwood to be the invoicing party (finance or compliance reasons). Also recommended for long-term reserved capacity deals.

Recommendation for Nimble Health (PoC -> scale):
- For the 2-month PoC (~$18k) we recommend Marketplace public listing if procurement wants the fastest route and doesn’t need custom legal terms.
- If you want a discounted pilot price or anticipate rolling the pilot into a committed reserved capacity (and want to keep unified cloud billing), opt for a Marketplace Private Offer — it balances speed and negotiated pricing.
- If Nimble plans for immediate reserved capacity, data isolation, or custom SLAs, pursue a direct MSA and PO (longer timeline, more flexible commercial terms).

Next steps I propose:
- If you want to move quickly: Proceed via public marketplace (I can provide the listing link and PO guidance).
- If you want discount and cloud-billing continuity: I’ll open the Private Offer path and coordinate with the AWS account team (I’ll need the procurement contact and PO template).
- If legal needs an MSA first: I’ll send a draft MSA and our standard SOW (DocuSign link below).

Links & attachments: pricing matrix and AWS Private Offer checklist attached. DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/link/to/draft-msa

Happy to jump on a 20-min call with procurement/legal to walk their teams through each lane — what time tomorrow works?

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0199
[Attachment stubs: Redwood_pricing_matrix_v3.pdf; AWS_private_offer_checklist.xlsx]

From: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Date: 2026-12-03T11:32:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Thanks Aditya — that breakdown helps. A couple quick clarifications:
- If we do a private offer via AWS, does the invoice still flow through AWS and satisfy our existing PO process (i.e., we get a single consolidated cloud invoice)?
- For direct Redwood invoicing, can Redwood accept a standard Nimble PO and net-30 terms? We typically require PO + net-30 for vendor payments.
- Support: for pilots we usually accept standard support, but if we commit to reserved capacity we will want a defined escalation path and a named TAM. Is that something Redwood can include in the MSA/SOW?

If we can get quick answers to the above, procurement can confirm a preference.

—Marty

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>, Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-05T17:48:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Marty — quick answers below and a recommended timeline: 

1) Private Offer invoicing: Yes — invoices are routed through AWS (or chosen cloud) and appear on your consolidated cloud bill. Private Offers allow custom pricing while keeping the cloud billing path intact. That satisfies most procurement PO reconciliation requirements.

2) Direct Redwood invoicing + PO: We can accept a Nimble PO and standard net-30 terms. We’ll include a payment clause in the MSA that aligns to PO processes. I’ve attached a draft MSA and SOW for your review (Redwood_MSA_draft_2026-12.docx).

3) Named TAM / support for reserved capacity: Yes — for committed/reserved customers we include a named Technical Account Manager and defined escalation SLAs in the SOW. We can scope response times and on-call windows in the MSA addendum.

Suggested timelines (typical):
- Public marketplace listing (purchase path ready): 1–7 business days from procurement approval.
- Marketplace Private Offer: 5–20 business days depending on cloud partner approvals and contract review.
- Direct MSA + PO (negotiation + signatures): 2–8 weeks depending on legal review depth.

If procurement wants to move fast, I recommend public marketplace for the PoC. If you want the pilot price locked and a clean transition to commitment while keeping your cloud billing, let me start the Private Offer workflow and I’ll loop in the AWS account team.

Next step: I’ll open the Private Offer intake if you confirm procurement contact and their PO template. Alternatively I can send the public listing link and a one-line PO sample for the quick path.

Best regards,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0199
[Attachments: Redwood_MSA_draft_2026-12.docx]
text:
From: Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>
Date: 2026-12-01T14:21:00Z
Subject: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Procurement is asking for a clear recommendation on whether we should buy Redwood through a cloud marketplace (AWS/Azure/GCP), request a Marketplace Private Offer, or go through a direct Redwood contract (MSA + PO). Their main concerns: speed of procurement, invoice and PO flow, discounts/credits, and any impact on support/SLA and co-sell benefits.

High-level context: Nimble Health is piloting a 2-month PoC (approx. $18k spend) and wants the option to scale to a reserved capacity commitment if things look good. The procurement team prefers marketplace buys for quick approval, but our legal team may request an MSA for anything above a 90-day pilot.

Can you outline pros/cons and practical timelines for each path? Also—any gotchas around co-sell credits or channel incentives we should be aware of?

Appreciate a short, actionable breakdown we can hand to procurement.

Thanks,
Samira
Samira Khan
Head of Procurement, Nimble Health
Samira.khan@nimblehealth.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

[Attachment stub: Nimble_SOW_draft.pdf]

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Samira Khan <samira.khan@nimblehealth.com>
Cc: Marty Jacobs <marty.jacobs@nimblehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-02T09:07:00Z
Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Samira — thanks for the clear context. Below is a concise, procurement-ready comparison and my recommendation based on Nimble's PoC -> scale profile. I’ve attached a short pricing matrix and a checklist we use for AWS Private Offers.

1) Cloud Marketplace (public listing)
- Speed: Fastest. Customer can purchase via existing cloud contract or consolidated invoice within 1–7 business days once procurement approves.
- Billing: Billed via the cloud provider’s invoice to the customer (consolidated cloud bill).
- Pricing & discounts: Standard list pricing on the listing. Limited ability to apply negotiated discounts — typically managed via Provider or Marketplace promotions, not line-item custom pricing.
- Support/SLA: Standard Redwood hosted SLA applies, but there are limits on contractual addenda via the marketplace. For pilots, this is often acceptable.
- Co-sell impact: Marketplace listing makes it straightforward for cloud partner teams to attribute usage, but co-sell incentive mechanics vary by partner and may require a private offer to capture special credits.
- Best when: Customer needs immediate procurement simplicity and is OK with standard pricing. Good for pilots and low-friction PoC purchases.

2) Marketplace Private Offer (MPP) / Cloud Private Offer
- Speed: Moderate. Setting up a private offer requires coordination between buyer procurement, cloud sales, and Redwood (usually 5–20 business days depending on approvals).
- Billing: Still billed through the cloud provider (customer sees charges on cloud bill), but pricing/discounts can be customized in the private offer.
- Pricing & discounts: Supports negotiated discounts, custom SKUs, and term-based commitments. Allows us to reflect a negotiated pilot price or scale pricing while keeping the billing path through the marketplace.
- Support/SLA: You can include a reference to the Redwood SLA, and commercial terms are captured in the private offer; however, legal teams sometimes still prefer an MSA for broader terms.
- Co-sell impact: Best path to preserve marketplace billing while enabling co-sell crediting and partner incentives — cloud partner needs to sign off on the private offer to make it co-sell-eligible.
- Best when: You want marketplace invoicing but need negotiated price/terms (pilot discounts, transition to commitment).

3) Direct Redwood Contract (MSA + PO / Redwood Invoice)
- Speed: Slowest. Negotiation of MSA and commercial terms typically 2–8 weeks (can be longer if custom legal terms).
- Billing: Redwood issues invoices directly (or can accept customer PO per the MSA).
- Pricing & discounts: Full flexibility to negotiate. Can include committed discounts, reserved capacity, and custom SLAs.
- Support/SLA: Full contract flexibility (dedicated support add-ons, explicit uptime targets, breach remedies).
- Co-sell impact: Co-sell crediting requires coordination; direct contracts can still be co-sell-enabled but may not flow through marketplace incentive systems automatically — we typically set a co-sell arrangement in parallel.
- Best when: Customer needs contractual protections, custom SLAs, or wants Redwood to be the invoicing party (finance or compliance reasons). Also recommended for long-term reserved capacity deals.

Recommendation for Nimble Health (PoC -> scale):
- For the 2-month PoC (~$18k) we recommend Marketplace public listing if procurement wants the fastest route and doesn’t need custom legal terms.
- If you want a discounted pilot price or anticipate rolling the pilot into a committed reserved capacity (and want to keep unified cloud billing), opt for a Marketplace Private Offer — it balances speed and negotiated pricing.
- If Nimble plans for immediate reserved capacity, data isolation, or custom SLAs, pursue a direct MSA and PO (longer timeline, more flexible commercial terms).

Next steps I propose:
- If you want to move quickly: Proceed via public marketplace (I can provide the listing link and PO guidance).
- If you want discount and cloud-billing continuity: I’ll open the Private Offer path and coordinate with the AWS account team (I’ll need the procurement contact and PO template).
- If legal needs an MSA first: I’ll send a draft MSA and our standard SOW (DocuSign link below).

Links & attachments: pricing matrix and AWS Private Offer checklist attached. DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/link/to/draft-msa

Happy to jump on a 20-min call with procurement/legal to walk their teams through each lane — what time tomorrow works?

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0199
[Attachment stubs: Redwood_pricing_matrix_v3.pdf; AWS_private_offer_checklist.xlsx]

- For direct Redwood invoicing, can Redwood accept a standard Nimble PO and net-30 terms? We typically require PO + net-30 for vendor payments.

Subject: Re: Procurement question — which purchase lane should we pick?

Marty — quick answers below and a recommended timeline: 

1) Private Offer invoicing: Yes — invoices are routed through AWS (or chosen cloud) and appear on your consolidated cloud bill. Private Offers allow custom pricing while keeping the cloud billing path intact. That satisfies most procurement PO reconciliation requirements.

2) Direct Redwood invoicing + PO: We can accept a Nimble PO and standard net-30 terms. We’ll include a payment clause in the MSA that aligns to PO processes. I’ve attached a draft MSA and SOW for your review (Redwood_MSA_draft_2026-12.docx).

3) Named TAM / support for reserved capacity: Yes — for committed/reserved customers we include a named Technical Account Manager and defined escalation SLAs in the SOW. We can scope response times and on-call windows in the MSA addendum.

Suggested timelines (typical):
- Public marketplace listing (purchase path ready): 1–7 business days from procurement approval.
- Marketplace Private Offer: 5–20 business days depending on cloud partner approvals and contract review.
- Direct MSA + PO (negotiation + signatures): 2–8 weeks depending on legal review depth.

If procurement wants to move fast, I recommend public marketplace for the PoC. If you want the pilot price locked and a clean transition to commitment while keeping your cloud billing, let me start the Private Offer workflow and I’ll loop in the AWS account team.

title: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path

Team —

We need to finalize a procurement path for our sales engagement tooling before the end of the quarter. Outcomes I want by next Friday: a license mapping (who stays on which product, seat counts), a recommended procurement route (renew Outreach vs. buy Salesloft or hybrid), and the expected all-in spend including transition costs.

Context: Outreach renewal is up Feb 2027. We also have a small Salesloft pilot with 30 seats that expires in Jan. Ops has flagged duplication of sequences and outbound tracking as the biggest inefficiency.

Please find attached a usage export and an initial license mapping I started (redwood_sales_engagement_usage_q3-q4_2026.xlsx, license_mapping_proposal_v2.pdf).

Ask: Marissa, lead vendor conversations and get updated quotes. Rishi, please validate the usage numbers and provide churn/seat growth assumptions. Laura (procurement), outline the PO and contracting timeline and whether we can bundle a single renewal/commit to get volume pricing.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_sales_engagement_usage_q3-q4_2026.xlsx, license_mapping_proposal_v2.pdf

From: Priya Shah <priya.shah@outreach.io>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: SalesOps Team <salesops@vendor.example.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 12:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path

Hi Marissa / Ben —

Thanks for the heads up and for sending the usage file. We can prepare a bundled proposal that includes a consolidated seat schedule, postage for data migration, and a 12-month price guarantee for committed seats. A couple of quick asks so our pricing is accurate:

- Can you confirm active monthly seat counts for Outreach for Oct/Nov? (total seats vs active users)
- Do you have an estimate for sequences that will be deprecated vs migrated? That affects migration effort.
- Are you looking for enterprise-level SSO + enhanced audit log retention (that changes the SKU)?

I can get a preliminary quote by EOD Thursday if you can share the clarifications. Happy to jump on a 30m call if useful.

Best,
Priya Shah
Strategic Accounts, Outreach

Attachments: none

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Priya Shah <priya.shah@outreach.io>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 2 Dec 2026 08:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path

Hi Priya —

Thanks — attached is a cleaned usage export with monthly active counts and a short note on sequences we plan to retire (redwood_sales_engagement_usage_q3-q4_2026.xlsx). Quick answers below:

- Outreach active seats (Oct/Nov): 420 and 435 respectively. Total seats provisioned: 500.
- We estimate ~90 sequences can be deprecated; ~150 will require light migration (templates + tagging).
- We do want enterprise SSO and audit logs retained for 12 months (security asked for 12-month retention).

We prefer a bundled approach that minimises dual-license time. Please include a line item for migration (hours and price), and any limits around API throughput that would impact our reporting.

Ben — looping you since procurement questions below.
Marissa
Senior Manager, GTM Ops

Attachments: redwood_sales_engagement_usage_q3-q4_2026.xlsx

From: Devon Hall <devon.hall@salesloft.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 3 Dec 2026 10:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path

Hi Ben / Marissa —

We heard you're consolidating and considering Salesloft for a larger footprint. Happy to share a detailed comparison and a migration playbook from Outreach. If you're open to a pilot extension, we can keep the 30-seat pilot active at no charge for one more quarter while we do a TCO comparison.

I'll follow up with a short template to capture what matters most to you (reporting, API throughput, sequence fidelity). Also: we can be price-competitive on seats if commitments are for 24 months.

Cheers,
Devon Hall
Head of Commercial, Salesloft

Attachments: none

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Priya Shah <priya.shah@outreach.io>, Devon Hall <devon.hall@salesloft.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 3 Dec 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path

Team —

Appreciate both Outreach and Salesloft replies. To keep the procurement runway short, here's the plan:

1) Priya — please send a line-item quote (seats, enterprise add-ons, migration hours) and a redlineable contract copy. If you can include a 10% volume discount for a 24-month commit and 30 days of overlap at no charge, that speeds approval.
2) Devon — thanks for the pilot extension offer. Can you provide a vendor-side cost estimate for a phased migration (30/150/420 seat phases) and confirm whether API limits will require a premium SKU?
3) Laura — please draft a PO template and list the earliest sign-off and PO dates you can meet if we pick Outreach vs Salesloft. Also flag any standard SLAs we should require.

Attached: procurement_requirements.docx (format template for vendor proposals). We'll reconvene Friday 10am to review quotes.

Marissa

Attachments: procurement_requirements.docx

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priya Shah <priya.shah@outreach.io>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 4 Dec 2026 16:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Seq stack license mapping & procurement path

Quick update — procurement timeline and ask:

- If we select Outreach and agree to a 24-month commit, Procurement can issue a PO within 7 business days from contract execution. We will require standard indemnities and an audit clause; please include data processing addenda for EU customers.
- For Salesloft, PO issuance is similar but vendor has to confirm SOC 2 Type II and the audit window; if Salesloft can provide that confirmation and a pilot-to-production migration plan, procurement can also hit a 10-business-day issuance.

preview:
From: Michael Liu <m.liu@acmehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Acme Health Legal <legal@acmehealth.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Private/on‑prem deployment — few legal asks

Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We're close on internal sign‑off but Legal flagged a few items in the MSA and the proposed Order Form for a private (on‑prem) deployment. High level asks:

- Confirm we can run an air‑gapped package if requested (they mention a narrow set of ops).
- License scope — they want a perpetual runtime license for the contracted model weights used for ACME workloads (rather than a time‑limited subscription).
- Audit rights — they want a quarterly access window to audit logs and to perform a technical audit for compliance; also a request for an escrow or source‑release-like mechanism if Redwood is acquired or goes offline.

Attached is an initial tracker of requested redlines from Legal. Can you loop in the right folks on your side? We'd like to capture feasibility and an initial timeline.

Thanks,
Michael
Michael Liu
Sr. Procurement Manager | Acme Health

Attachment: Acme_Redlines_20261130.xlsx (spreadsheet stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Michael Liu <m.liu@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Nov 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Private/on‑prem deployment — few legal asks

Michael — appreciate the clear list. Looping in Neha (Legal) and Laura (Sales‑Ops) to validate what we can commit to in the MSA and Order Form. Quick notes from my side:

- Air‑gapped: we have an on‑prem package that can be run without outbound network access; ops handoff and packaging take ~3–4 weeks from agreement.
- Perpetual runtimes: that's an enterprise legal ask that isn't our default model — we need to confirm licensing and pricing impact.
- Audit windows & logs: we can provide audit logs and a read‑only export; we need to define retention and scope.
- Escrow: we do not typically release source, but we can discuss an escrow arrangement or a defined contingency (maintenance SLA + support runway).

Neha — can you review Michael's tracker and flag what we can accept as standard redlines vs. items requiring exec approval? Laura — once we have legal guidance I'll produce an updated Order Form and an estimated SOW/ops timeline.

Next step: internal legal review by EOD Wed, then a call with Acme to walk redlines on Thursday? Thoughts?

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
Phone: (415) 555‑0127
--
Attachments: none

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Michael Liu <m.liu@acmehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 08:47:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Private/on‑prem deployment — few legal asks

Thanks Ben — I reviewed Acme's tracker (Acme_Redlines_20261130.xlsx). Summary of redline positions and recommended responses: 

1) Perpetual license for model weights: REDWOOD POSITION — we can offer a long‑term perpetual runtime license under a separate commercial addendum but with conditions: (a) license covers a specific model version(s) verified at delivery, (b) we retain IP in model weights and only license runtime usage for ACME workloads, (c) commercial premium applies. Require commercial signoff and procurement approval.

2) Air‑gapped/on‑prem package: OK — we will deliver an on‑prem artifact and runbook; require acceptance testing window and a Handover SOW.

3) Audit rights & logs: we can provide audit log exports and a quarterly audit window; audit to be limited to verifying ACME data handling and compliance; for security reasons we will not provide broad internal access. Suggest a Mirrored‑Site Read‑Only export and an agreed scope doc.

4) Escrow/source release: we do not support code escrow for runtime kernels or proprietary infra. Alternative: (a) enhanced continuity SLA with financial remedies and (b) a third‑party escrow for critical configuration + verified model artifacts (not source). If Acme insists on full source escrow that will require executive approval and legal negotiation.

5) Insurance/compliance evidence: they requested SOC2 artifacts — provide SOC2 Type II report under NDA or via secure portal.

I'll prepare redline language (MSA addendum + Order Form note) and mark the clauses that require commercial/exec approval. Ben — is commercial ok to price the perpetual option? Laura — can you estimate uplift (ballpark %) for a perpetual license and for the escrow substitute?

-Neha
Neha Kapoor
Senior Counsel | Redwood Inference

Cc: Michael Liu <m.liu@acmehealth.com>
Date: Wed, 2 Dec 2026 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Private/on‑prem deployment — few legal asks

Quick commercial thoughts — preliminary estimates only: 

- Perpetual runtime license: 3–4x uplift on the standard 12‑month committed price (reflects transfer of maintenance/continuity risk). We should present a limited‑term perpetual option (perpetual license + annual maintenance fee ~15%).
- Escrow/continuity package: a one‑time project fee to set up a managed continuity binder + third‑party escrow deposit (~$40k) plus a monthly continuity support fee.
- On‑prem delivery: billable SOW for packaging, validation and handover; estimate 3–6 weeks and ~$45k–$85k depending on environment complexity.

I'll create an Order Form variant (redlined) that includes these options and an itemized SOW. Ben — I can join the redlines call and present commercial tradeoffs.

Laura Bennett
Director, Sales Operations | Redwood Inference

To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: Private/on‑prem deployment — few legal asks

Thanks all — helpful summary. A few clarifying notes from Legal: 

- Perpetual license is a hard requirement for our Board for the mission‑critical model. They prefer the perpetual + annual maintenance approach you suggested.
- For audit, they want confirmation we can schedule a 4‑hour technical audit with their SOC team and receive the relevant logs for the prior 90 days. They do not require any internal access beyond read‑only exports.
- Escrow: they'd accept the third‑party escrow for model artifacts as an alternative to full source, provided the escrow includes a mechanism to release artifacts if Redwood becomes insolvent.

When is a good time for a 30‑minute call to go over the proposed Order Form variants early next week? Also attaching a redline from Acme Legal to help speed the review.

-Michael

title: Billing allocation and ASC 606 checklist for multi-year prepay
preview:
From: Elena Campos <elena@britech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Britech Procurement <procure@britech.com>
Cc: Tom Harris <tharris@britech.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Billing approach for 3-year prepay (ASC 606 questions)

Hi Ben / team,

We just received the countersigned SOW for the 3-year prepay. Our auditors are asking for a few items before we book the commitment: a clear invoice schedule/wording they can match to our ledger, and a revenue recognition memo that explains how Redwood will allocate revenue across the three years under ASC 606.

Two specific asks from finance:
- Can Redwood issue invoices split by fiscal year (we prefer a separate invoice for each contract year), or will you issue one upfront invoice and provide an allocation schedule?
- We need sample invoice wording and GL line detail (service start date, term, applied discounts) so it aligns with our SAP mapping.

I've attached the signed SOW (britech-signed-sow.pdf). Can you confirm next steps and timeline — our month-end close is coming up.

Thanks,
Elena Campos
Director, Finance — Britech
Attachment: britech-signed-sow.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Campos <elena@britech.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 12:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Billing approach for 3-year prepay (ASC 606 questions)

Elena — thanks for the note and congrats on getting the SOW signed. TL;DR: we can support both approaches and will provide the auditor materials you need. Details below and an attached allocation workbook.

Options we support:
1) Separate fiscal-year invoices: Redwood can generate three invoices that correspond to each contract year (Invoice YYYY-01, YYYY-02, YYYY-03). Customer pays per invoice when issued; this is simplest for your AR/GL mapping.
2) One upfront invoice with split allocation: Redwood invoices the full prepay now and supplies an allocation schedule and an ASC 606 memo for your auditors. We then recognize revenue over the contract period in our ledger — for you the payment is a prepayment.

What we included in the attached proposed-allocation.xlsx: a simple year-by-year breakdown (amounts, discount allocation, suggested invoice descriptions). We modeled two allocation methods: straight-line over term, and usage-weighted (if you expect ramp).

Next steps I suggest:
- Marcus (finance) to confirm preferred invoice flow and any required invoice fields.
- We will produce a short ASC 606 memo + recognition schedule for your audit team.
- If you want separate invoices, confirm invoice recipients and PO number(s).

title: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path
preview:
From: Marta Ruiz <marta@pinecone.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path

Hi Ben,

Following our call last week, the Pinecone integrations team put together a short proposal for a lightweight \"mirroring accelerator\" that keeps an indexed slice in Pinecone warmed from an S3-backed embedding pipeline. The goal is to reduce cold-query tail latency for infrequent indexes while preserving a single canonical storage layer in S3.

Attached: rnw-mirror-design_v1.pdf (high-level flow) and sample-index-schema.json.

Key asks from our side to scope a pilot: 
- Expected write QPS (ingest) and read QPS for the index during peak  (we used a 50qps baseline in the doc).
- Retention window for hot slice (hours/days) and whether you require explicit deletion hooks.
- Compliance constraints (any EU-only residency or encryption-at-rest policies).
- Success criteria for the spike: 95th p50/p95 latency targets, and parity with your current cold-hit rate.

We can run a 2-week tech spike where Pinecone provides a small test cluster and a connector; Redwood provides samples and a perf harness. If that sounds OK, can we schedule a 30-min deep dive next week to align on the test harness and data sets?

Thanks,
Marta Ruiz
Partner Solutions
Pinecone

Attachments: rnw-mirror-design_v1.pdf (8 pages, diagram + flow), sample-index-schema.json (2KB)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Marta Ruiz <marta@pinecone.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 11:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path

Marta — thanks for the proposal and the artifacts. Short answers up front:

- Baseline traffic: we expect ~20 write QPS during ingestion windows for the pilot index and 40 read QPS with spikes to ~120qps during business hours for the small retail dataset.
- Hot slice retention: target 6 hours for our initial run, with an option to extend to 24 hours if cost/latency tradeoffs look favorable.
- Residency: pilot will run in us-west; production customers may have eu constraints but not for this PoC.

Success criteria we care about: p50 < 30ms, p95 < 150ms for single-vector nearest-neighbor queries on the warmed slice, and less than 5% increase in top-K mismatch vs baseline.

Operational asks: 
- Can Pinecone expose token-level per-minute metrics for the warmed slice (qps, cached hit-rate, cpu/gpu util)?
- We need a simple fail-open fallback to S3 search index if cluster availability drops below 99.5% for 10 mins.

LO: Laura, could you own the procurement/legal touchpoints? Karthik, can you and your infra team review the design doc and indicate any blockers from our side?

If folks are good with Tuesday 12/8 at 10:00 PT, I can lock a calendar invite.

— Ben
Ben Carter
Partner Sales
Redwood Inference

---
On Tue, 1 Dec 2026 at 09:15, Marta Ruiz <marta@pinecone.io> wrote:
> Following our call last week, the Pinecone integrations team put together a short proposal for a lightweight \"mirroring accelerator\"...


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Cc: Marta Ruiz <marta@pinecone.io>
Date: Wed, 2 Dec 2026 08:28:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path

Ben/Laura — skimmed the design doc and added a few technical notes here. High-level: the flow looks feasible but we need to validate two things in the spike: schema compatibility with our embedding producer and the checkpointing semantics for partial index flushes.

Questions / notes:
- Schema: sample-index-schema.json maps to our current embedding vector dims (1536) but the doc uses a 1024 example in places; let's standardize to 1536 to avoid silent mismatches.
- Checkpoint semantics: we need idempotent syncs. If Pinecone accepts a write-id/version header we can avoid replay duplicates.
- Cold-to-hot promotion: suggest we implement a TTL-based bloom-check on our side to avoid re-warming indexes that will be evicted anyway.

I've started a JIRA ticket: SUP-2345 (mirror-accelerator-spike). Dropped a perf harness skeleton and a small perf matrix (attached).

Attaching: perf_matrix.csv (microbench numbers we want to capture: single-vector p50/p95, batch write throughput, snapshot restore time).

Karthik
Sr. Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: perf_matrix.csv (1 sheet)

> On Tue, 1 Dec 2026 at 11:02, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Marta — thanks for the proposal and the artifacts...


From: Marta Ruiz <marta@pinecone.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Pinecone Integrations Team <integrations@pinecone.io>
Date: Thu, 3 Dec 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path

Thanks — this is helpful. Tuesday 12/8 10:00 PT works for me and the integration engineers; I sent a calendar invite to the group.

Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path

Hi Ben,

Following our call last week, the Pinecone integrations team put together a short proposal for a lightweight \"mirroring accelerator\" that keeps an indexed slice in Pinecone warmed from an S3-backed embedding pipeline. The goal is to reduce cold-query tail latency for infrequent indexes while preserving a single canonical storage layer in S3.

Attached: rnw-mirror-design_v1.pdf (high-level flow) and sample-index-schema.json.

Key asks from our side to scope a pilot: 
- Expected write QPS (ingest) and read QPS for the index during peak  (we used a 50qps baseline in the doc).
- Retention window for hot slice (hours/days) and whether you require explicit deletion hooks.
- Compliance constraints (any EU-only residency or encryption-at-rest policies).
- Success criteria for the spike: 95th p50/p95 latency targets, and parity with your current cold-hit rate.

We can run a 2-week tech spike where Pinecone provides a small test cluster and a connector; Redwood provides samples and a perf harness. If that sounds OK, can we schedule a 30-min deep dive next week to align on the test harness and data sets?

Thanks,
Marta Ruiz
Partner Solutions
Pinecone

Attachments: rnw-mirror-design_v1.pdf (8 pages, diagram + flow), sample-index-schema.json (2KB)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Marta Ruiz <marta@pinecone.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 11:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Compact index mirroring accelerator — scope & QA path

Marta — thanks for the proposal and the artifacts. Short answers up front:

- Baseline traffic: we expect ~20 write QPS during ingestion windows for the pilot index and 40 read QPS with spikes to ~120qps during business hours for the small retail dataset.
- Hot slice retention: target 6 hours for our initial run, with an option to extend to 24 hours if cost/latency tradeoffs look favorable.
- Residency: pilot will run in us-west; production customers may have eu constraints but not for this PoC.

Success criteria we care about: p50 < 30ms, p95 < 150ms for single-vector nearest-neighbor queries on the warmed slice, and less than 5% increase in top-K mismatch vs baseline.

Operational asks: 
- Can Pinecone expose token-level per-minute metrics for the warmed slice (qps, cached hit-rate, cpu/gpu util)?
- We need a simple fail-open fallback to S3 search index if cluster availability drops below 99.5% for 10 mins.

LO: Laura, could you own the procurement/legal touchpoints? Karthik, can you and your infra team review the design doc and indicate any blockers from our side?

If folks are good with Tuesday 12/8 at 10:00 PT, I can lock a calendar invite.

— Ben
Ben Carter
Partner Sales
Redwood Inference

title: Billing setup: delivery pathway preference, consolidation, and PO mapping
preview:
From: Elena Morrell <elena@brightline.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.example>
Cc: procurement@brightline.com
Date: 2026-12-02T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Billing onboarding — delivery & consolidation preference

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. We're ready to lock down how invoices will be sent once we go live. A few constraints from our AP team:

- Brightline prefers a single, consolidated invoice per month per legal entity (one PDF for the month) rather than daily invoicing.
- Primary technical preference is to receive invoices via our AP portal (we can accept API push or SFTP ingest). If you push to the portal, we also need an email copy to finance@brightline.com for our records.
- We need VAT registration evidence on file (attached VAT certificate).
- Please map invoices to PO GF-9876 for the initial rollout. I've attached the signed PO.

Can Redwood support: a portal push + email-copy workflow and produce a consolidated monthly PDF plus an electronic e-invoice XML file for our AP system? Also let me know the billing contact you need set on the account and any sample file names/format you require.

Goal: go-live the week of Dec 7th if possible — does that timeline work?

Thanks,
Elena Morrell
Brightline — Head of Procurement
(attachment: Brightline_PO_GF-9876.pdf)
(attachment: Brightline_VAT_Certificate.pdf)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.example>
To: Elena Morrell <elena@brightline.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.example>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.example>
Date: 2026-12-02T10:02:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Billing onboarding — delivery & consolidation preference

Hi Elena — great to see the PO, and thanks for the clear preferences. Short answers up front: yes, we can do portal push + email copy, and we can produce a consolidated monthly PDF invoice plus an accompanying e-invoice XML.

Options we can offer (pick one):
1) Portal push (preferred): Redwood pushes the invoice artefacts to your AP portal via webhook/API or SFTP. We will also simultaneously email finance@brightline.com with the consolidated PDF.
2) Email-first fallback: we email the consolidated PDF to finance@brightline.com and upload an XML to a secure SFTP.

Notes / requirements from our side:
- We need a billing contact name and email to show on invoices (we can set that in the account settings to \"Brightline AP <finance@brightline.com>\").
- Please confirm PO mapping rules: should every invoice line be tagged GF-9876 or only the first invoice for the period?
- For VAT evidence, your VAT certificate is sufficient — we attach it to the customer record.
- We'll provide a sample e-invoice XML schema and filename convention (attached).

Next steps: Marissa (Billing Ops) / Kimberly (Sales Finance) are looped to confirm the exact XML/SFTP parameters and to schedule a short technical handoff call. If you want the DocuSign amendment for billing/portal delivery, we can send that once you confirm the preferred option.

I'll pencil in Dec 7th for a soft go-live pending the SFTP/API details — let us know if you need an earlier smoke test.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
redwood.example | +1 415-555-0123
(attachment: redwood_onboarding_checklist.pdf)
(attachment: sample_einvoice_schema.xml)
